subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
title: Biological Diversity (Access and Benefit Sharing) Regulation 2025
focus: Key highlights of the 2025 ABS regulations, including Digital Sequence Information (DSI), Prior Informed Consent (PIC), and benefit-sharing slabs.
rationale: The 2025 regulation introduces new concepts like DSI and specific financial slabs for benefit sharing, which are highly testable in UPSC prelims.
sectionExcerpt: Notified by the National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) to replace 2014 Guidelines. Includes Digital Sequence Information (DSI) as a mode of benefit sharing. Requires Prior Informed Consent (PIC) for accessing biological resources. Slabs based on annual turnover range from nil (up to 5 Crore) to 0.6% (above 250 Crore). High-value resources require at least 5% of auction/sale proceeds.
evidenceBullets: Notified by NBA to replace 2014 Guidelines under BDA 2002.
Includes Digital Sequence Information (DSI) - digital representations of genetic resources like DNA/RNA.
Exempts access to cultivated medicinal plants notified under BDA 2002.
Benefit sharing slabs range from nil (up to 5 Cr turnover) to 0.6% (above 250 Cr turnover).
High-Value Biological Resources (e.g., red sanders) require minimum 5% of auction/sale proceeds.

subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
title: International Framework: CBD, Bonn Guidelines, and Nagoya Protocol
focus: The Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) and its associated protocols and guidelines governing Access and Benefit Sharing globally.
rationale: International environmental conventions and their specific protocols (Nagoya, Cartagena) are staple UPSC questions.
sectionExcerpt: The Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) is a 1992 Rio Earth Summit treaty with 196 members, including India. Its goals are conservation, sustainable use, and fair benefit-sharing. Major protocols include Cartagena (Biosafety) and Nagoya (ABS). The Bonn Guidelines (2002) are voluntary for ABS, while the Nagoya Protocol (2010) is legally binding.
evidenceBullets: CBD adopted at 1992 Rio Earth Summit; India is a member.
Three main goals of CBD: Conservation, Sustainable use, Fair and Equitable Benefit-Sharing.
Cartagena Protocol (2003) deals with Biosafety.
Nagoya Protocol (2010) is a legally-binding agreement for fair and equitable sharing of benefits from genetic resources.
Bonn Guidelines (2002) are voluntary guidelines for ABS.
Multilateral Mechanism for DSI use adopted at CBD COP16 (2024).

subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
title: India's Legal Framework on ABS (Biological Diversity Act, 2002)
focus: Statutory framework under the BD Act 2002, its three-tier implementation system, and notable case studies.
rationale: The three-tier institutional structure (NBA, SBB, BMC) and the scope of the BD Act are critical for polity and environment governance questions.
sectionExcerpt: The Biological Diversity Act, 2002 covers all biological resources within India, broader than the Nagoya Protocol's scope. It features a three-tier statutory system: National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) with civil court powers, State Biodiversity Boards (SBBs), and Biodiversity Management Committees (BMCs) at the local level. A key case study is the Kani tribal community's benefit sharing for the Jeevani drug from the Arogyapacha plant.
evidenceBullets: BD Act 2002 covers all biological resources in India, which is broader than the Nagoya Protocol.
Implemented via a three-tier system: NBA (Central), SBBs (State), and BMCs (Local).
National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) possesses the powers of a civil court.
Kani tribal community of Kerala shares benefits from the Jeevani drug developed from the Arogyapacha plant.

subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
title: CSS-Integrated Development of Wildlife Habitats Scheme (CSS-IDWH)
focus: Scheme details, nodal ministry, funding structure, and the Species Recovery Programme.
rationale: Centrally Sponsored Schemes related to environment, especially those funding protected areas and species recovery, are frequently tested.
sectionExcerpt: CSS-IDWH is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme under MoEFCC for wildlife protection. It provides financial assistance to State/UTs for Protected Areas, wildlife outside protected areas, and recovery programmes for critically endangered species. 22 species are included, with Gharial and Sloth Bear recently recommended. Key components include Development of Wildlife Habitats, Project Tiger, and Project Elephant.
evidenceBullets: Centrally Sponsored Scheme under the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC).
Funds Protected Areas, wildlife outside PAs, and critically endangered species recovery programmes.
22 species currently included, such as Snow Leopard, Asiatic Lion, and Gangetic River Dolphin.
Gharial and Sloth Bear recommended for inclusion in the Species Recovery Programme.
Key components include Project Tiger and Project Elephant.

subtopic: Biological Diversity Act and ABS Regulations
title: Species Profile: Sloth Bear
focus: Characteristics, habitat, threats, and conservation status of the Sloth Bear.
rationale: Species profiles with specific IUCN/WPA statuses and unique behavioral traits (e.g., non-hibernating, myrmecophagous) are standard UPSC prelims material.
sectionExcerpt: The Sloth Bear specializes in eating termites and ants and does not hibernate. It is solitary, nocturnal, and native to India, Nepal, and Sri Lanka. Its conservation status is Schedule I under WPA 1972, Appendix I under CITES, and Vulnerable (VU) under IUCN. Daroji Sloth Bear Sanctuary in Karnataka is Asia's first dedicated sloth bear sanctuary.
evidenceBullets: Specializes in eating termites and ants and does not hibernate unlike other bears.
Native to forests and grasslands of India, Nepal, and Sri Lanka.
Conservation Status: WPA 1972 Schedule I, CITES Appendix I, IUCN Vulnerable (VU).
Daroji Sloth Bear Sanctuary in Karnataka is the first dedicated sloth bear sanctuary in Asia.
Project Sloth Bear initiated in 2025.
